coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dot/activated carbon nanotube-supported copper nanoparticles as an efficient electrocatalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Jaemun
Noh, Sunguk
Shim, Jun Ho
coredata.dc:description: Recent decades have witnessed remarkable progress in fuel cell technology for efficient energy conversion devices in stationary, automotive, and mobile systems. However, the choice of the electrode material in the fuel cell technologies is still limited to platinum and the other platinum group metals, necessitating further research for alternatives. Herein, activated carbon nanotubes (ACNTs) treated at a high temperature (500 °C) were used as a support to induce binding with N-doped carbon dots (NCDs). Cu(II) in the copper nanoparticles spontaneously reduced to Cu(0) using the NCDs as a reducing agent; thus, a Cu/NCD/ACNT hybrid catalyst was synthesized. The oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) activity of the Cu/NCD/ACNT catalyst (E onset = 0.921 V) was higher than those of Cu/ACNT (E onset = 0.821 V) and NCD/ACNT (E onset = 0.869 V) catalysts. The Cu/NCD/ACNT catalyst also exhibited excellent tolerance to methanol, in relation to Pt/C, during a methanol fuel cell tolerance test. Moreover, the current decays after 10 h in the ORR durability test were 21% and 10% for the Pt/C and Cu/NCD/ACNT catalysts, respectively, demonstrating the greater durability of the non-metal catalyst. Thus, the Cu/NCD/ACNT catalyst, formed by the synergistic action of ACNT, N doping, and spontaneous reduction of Cu(II), is a promising alternative to commercial Pt/C catalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dot
Activated carbon nanotube
Copper nanoparticle
Oxygen reduction reaction
Fuel cell

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Daegu University Research Grant, 2020.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) pristine CNTs (acid purification treatment with concentrated HNO3:H2SO4 (1:3, %v/v)) and (b) ACNTs. (c) BET surface areas (inset: pore-size distributions) and (d) Raman spectra (inset: enlarged spectra of G bands) showing different degrees of structural distortion of CNTs and ACNTs.
TEM images of (a) NCD, (b) NCD/ACNT, (c) Cu/ACNT, and (d) Cu/NCD/ACNT. (e-i) TEM-EDS elemental mapping (scale bar: 300 nm) of Cu/NCD/ACNT.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of Cu/ACNT, NCD/ACNT, and Cu/NCD/ACNT.
Deconvoluted XPS spectra of (a) survey, (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, and (d) Cu 2p: (A) Cu/ACNT, (B) NCD/ACNT, and (C) Cu/NCD/ACNT.

(a and b) Electrocatalytic ORR performance of different samples in an O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 (rotation rate = 1600 rpm). The voltammograms were obtained from the RRDE-disk curves of each catalyst. (c) Electrocatalytic RRDE-ring voltammograms for the yield of peroxide produced by the 2-electron reduction of oxygen in an O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 (rotation rate = 1600 rpm). (d) Electron transfer numbers (left axis) and peroxide yields (right axis) obtained from the RRDE curves of each catalyst. (e) Plots of the capacitive current density (Δj in 0.96 V vs. RHE) as a function of the scan rate. (f) Chronoamperometric responses at 0.8 V (vs. RHE) in a N2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution with the addition of 1.0 M methanol at 50 s.

(a) EIS measurements of the different catalysts for the ORR in an O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at the potential of 0.8 V vs. RHE (rotation rate = 1600 rpm). (b) Chronoamperometric i-t response of Cu/NCD/ACNT, NCD/ACNT and Pt/C in an O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at 400 rpm at the potential of 0.5 V (vs. RHE) for 600 min. The measured j values were converted into j percentages.
Electrocatalytic ORR characteristics of the catalysts.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dot/activated carbon nanotube-supported copper nanoparticles as an efficient electrocatalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Daegu University, Gyeongsan 38453, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Daegu University, Gyeongsan 38453, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A nitrogen-doped and activated carbon structure-supported Cu/NCD/ACNT hybrid catalyst exhibits excellent catalytic activity, superior stability, and methanol tolerance compared to a commercial Pt/C catalyst.

Recent decades have witnessed remarkable progress in fuel cell technology for efficient energy conversion devices in stationary, automotive, and mobile systems. However, the choice of the electrode material in the fuel cell technologies is still limited to platinum and the other platinum group metals, necessitating further research for alternatives. Herein, activated carbon nanotubes (ACNTs) treated at a high temperature (500 °C) were used as a support to induce binding with N-doped carbon dots (NCDs). Cu(II) in the copper nanoparticles spontaneously reduced to Cu(0) using the NCDs as a reducing agent; thus, a Cu/NCD/ACNT hybrid catalyst was synthesized. The oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) activity of the Cu/NCD/ACNT catalyst (E onset = 0.921 V) was higher than those of Cu/ACNT (E onset = 0.821 V) and NCD/ACNT (E onset = 0.869 V) catalysts. The Cu/NCD/ACNT catalyst also exhibited excellent tolerance to methanol, in relation to Pt/C, during a methanol fuel cell tolerance test. Moreover, the current decays after 10 h in the ORR durability test were 21% and 10% for the Pt/C and Cu/NCD/ACNT catalysts, respectively, demonstrating the greater durability of the non-metal catalyst. Thus, the Cu/NCD/ACNT catalyst, formed by the synergistic action of ACNT, N doping, and spontaneous reduction of Cu(II), is a promising alternative to commercial Pt/C catalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: A fuel cell is an environmentally friendly energy system that exhibits little energy loss and emits H2O, heat, and oxygen as byproducts of the electricity generation pathway. Owing to the recent exponential increase in energy consumption, renewable energy has attracted the attention of many researchers [1,2]. Of the various types of fuel cells, alkaline fuel cells (AFCs) offer several advantages such as a high electrical efficiency, easy to handle, ability to operate at low temperatures (60–70 °C), and simple structure. Hence, AFCs have been actively studied owing to their potential application in future automobile systems and as the main power source for households. A half-cell reaction occurs in AFCs; in the hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR), a hydrogen molecule (H2) is oxidized to generate water and electrons (H2 + 2OH− → 2H2O + 2e−) at the anode, while in the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), an oxygen molecule (O2) reacts with water and electrons to hydroxide ions (O2 + 2H2O + 4e− → 4OH−) at the cathode [3-5]. The ORR at the cathode, unlike the HOR at the anode, involves a complex four-electron transfer reaction, which generates an overpotential and results in slow kinetic reactions, thereby reducing the overall efficiency of AFCs [6-8]. This is primarily attributed to the high binding energy (498 kJ mol−1) between oxygen molecules (OO). Consequently, the development of a catalyst that can lower this activation energy is one of the key research topics in fuel cell technology.

The currently employed commercialized fuel cell electrode catalysts use Pt-based noble metals. Pt has low economic feasibility and is highly scarce, making it unsuitable for mass production and commercialization [8,9]. In Pt-based direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs), long-term operation of the fuel cell can lead to a methanol cross-over effect and decrease the overall performance of the DMFC [4,10]. Hence, alternative non-precious metal catalysts with excellent electrocatalytic activities and high abundance have been explored. Most studies on non-precious metal catalysts have focused on transition metals and materials containing transition metals. These include transition metal chalcogenides such as cobalt sulfides, which are a combination of Co and S, a chalcogen element [11-13], transition metal oxides (oxidized form of transition metals) [14-16], and nitrides (a form of N-doped transition metal) [17-19]. These studies demonstrated the potential of using various combinations of transition metals as new catalyst materials. Research on Fe [20], Co [21], and Mn [22] elements has also been actively conducted. Although comparatively limited, several studies involving Cu have also been conducted [23,24]. Cu-based nanomaterials are well-known for their electrochemical catalytic activities in applications other than fuel cells, such as glucose oxidation [25,26], H2O2 oxidation/reduction [27], and methanol oxidation [28].

A pure transition metal-based catalyst has higher activation barrier of the reaction, which results in a low ORR catalytic performance and renders the catalyst unsuitable as an alternative to Pt-based catalysts [29,30]. To overcome this problem, many studies have attempted to use electroconductive materials as the support structure. For instance, Yan et al. reported that a Cu2O/reduced graphene oxide (RGO) material, synthesized by dispersing Cu2O nanoparticles on an RGO support, showed high catalytic performance and methanol resistance [31]. Dai et al. synthesized a Co3O4/G catalyst using Co3O4 nanocrystals and graphene as the carbon structure [21]. The synthesized catalyst showed superior ORR catalytic performance to that of Co3O4 nanocrystals without the carbon structure. Thus, the addition of carbon supports to Co3O4 nanocrystals improves the rate of electron transfer and has synergistic effects on the catalytic activity, thereby accelerating the kinetic response during the ORR [21].

In general, carbon structures with high thermal and chemical stabilities are a research focus in the field of non-precious metal-based electrocatalysts. Typical carbon support materials include grapheme [32-35], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [36-38], nano-/meso-porous carbons [39,40], and carbon dots (CDs) [41,42]. In addition to high thermal and chemical stabilities and excellent electrical conductivity, carbon supports are also easy to modify and have a large surface area. CDs function as excellent Cu-reducing agents [43]. Hence, in this study, CNTs and CDs, which are popular catalyst and carbon support materials, respectively, were used to form a hybrid structure with the transition metal Cu. Pristine CNTs obtained by removing all residual metals from the nano-carbon manufacturing process were used. Activated carbon nanotubes (ACNTs) were prepared by heat treatment (500 °C, 1 h) in air to enlarge the surface area of the CNTs and the catalytic sites. Ethylenediamine was used as the N dopant for the CDs to synthesize N-doped CDs (NCDs). Thus, the spontaneous synthesis of Cu nanostructures was achieved in the presence of NCDs as reducing agents. Finally, a Cu/NCD/ACNT hybrid catalyst with excellent ORR catalytic activity as a fuel cell cathode was synthesized. The catalytic performance of this catalyst was comparable to that of Pt/C while overcoming the shortcomings of Pt, and is expected to be an alternative to commercial Pt/C catalysts.

Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs, ≥98% carbon) and CuCl2·2H2O were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich·H2O2 (28%) and ethylenediamine anhydrous used for NCD synthesis were supplied by Duksan company. Commercial Pt/C (20 wt% Pt loading on Vulcan XC-72) used as the comparator was obtained from Premetek. All the aqueous solutions were prepared with triple distilled water. The structural and morphological properties of synthesized samples were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philip, CM-200). The functional groups of the sample surface and their properties were analyzed through Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Nicolet iS5). The surface structure and chemical properties of the catalyst were analyzed by Raman spectroscopy (HORIBA, XploRa plus) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS Nova, AlKα monochromatized radiation). The crystallinity of the sample was measured by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku D/max-2500, λ = 1.54 Å, Cu-Kα radiation). The Cu contents in the Cu/NCD/ACNT catalyst were analyzed by inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES, Optima 7300 DV, Perkin Elmer). The solid surface area of the CNT was analyzed by Brunauer–Emmett–Teller analysis (BET, BEL SORPmini II BEL Japan, Inc.).

All electrochemical measurements were performed using a standard three-electrode system. Glassy carbon (GC) electrode, saturated calomel electrode (SCE), and GC rod were used as the working, reference, and counter electrodes, respectively. The working electrode was wet-polished with alumina slurries and sonicated in distilled water to remove residual aluminum slurries. Each prepared catalyst sample was dispersed in distilled water at a concentration of 2.0 mg mL−1. Thereafter, 5 μL of the suspension was repeatedly dropped onto the GC electrode (0.071 cm2, average catalyst loading of 0.42 mg cm−2) thrice and dried at room temperature. Then, 5 μL of Nafion (0.05 wt%) was dropped onto the GC electrode coated with the sample to prevent separation of the sample from the electrode. All electrochemical ORR measurements were performed in an O2– or N2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution. Measured potentials were converted to reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) scale using the Nernst equation: E(RHE) = E(SCE) + 0.059 × pH + 0.241. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed with CHI 601 (CH instruments) within −0.1 and 1.2 V (vs. RHE). Linear-sweep voltammetry within 0.3 and 1.1 V (vs. RHE) at a series of rotating speeds of 400, 900, 1600, 2500, and 3600 rpm was performed either by a rotating disk electrode (RDE, BAS Bioanalytical System) or rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE-3A, ALS Inc).

N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized using a one-step thermal synthesis process [44]. First, 10 mL of ethylenediamine anhydrous was added to a beaker (250 mL), and 10 mL of H2O2 was added dropwise while stirring. The color of the solution turned pale yellow and heat was generated. The mixture was then heated at 200 °C for 7 min until a dark brown colloid NCD solution was formed. The suspension was washed using a 3.5 K molecular weight cut-off dialysis membrane to remove impurities and obtain homogeneous suspension of NCDs.

The CNTs were purified in HNO3:H2SO4 (1:3, %v/v) solution for 12 h to remove any residual metals and impurities before use. The purified CNTs were rinsed and filtered repeatedly under ambient condition with distilled water until the pH became ≥ 6.0. To increase surface area and catalytic activity, the CNTs were then placed in a muffle furnace with a heating rate increasing to 500 °C at 5 °C min−1 for 1 h. The pretreated CNTs (ACNTs) were stored in a desiccator requiring constant temperature and humidity.

Herein, 10 mg of the prepared ACNTs was added to a three-neck distilling flask containing 10 mL of distilled water, followed by sonication for 1 h for uniform dispersion. Then, 10 mmol of CuCl2·2H2O was added to the mixture and sonicated for an additional 30 min, followed by the addition of 10 mL of prepared NCDs. The mixture was heated under reflux at 100 °C for 2 h. After the reaction, the suspension was centrifuged and washed with distilled water several times. Cu/ACNT and NCD/ACNT used for comparisons were prepared by the same methods, and the total volume of the mixture during the synthesis process was kept uniform using distilled water. Each prepared sample was dried at 50 °C for 6 h in a desiccator. Subsequently, the samples were calcinated at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 increasing to 800 °C for 2 h in a tube furnace with N2 gas.

Carbon nanostructures (CNTs, graphene, and CDs, among others) are preferred in many electrocatalysts owing to their excellent physical and chemical properties [35,36,41]. Washing pristine CNTs with strong acid formed wrinkles and defects in the outer walls, which were observed partially (Fig. 1 a). In contrast, pretreatment of CNTs at high temperatures generated wrinkles and defects in the inner walls. which could be observed clearly (Fig. 1b). Broken carbon walls were also observed in certain regions of the CNTs (Fig. 1b, inset). Defects on the carbon nanostructure increase surface area and active sites for the ORR, thereby contributing toward the improvements in electron transfer and reaction rate. Fig. 1c shows the BET surface area measurements of CNT before and after thermal pretreatment, which were classified as type IV isotherms representing mesoporous structures [45,46]. The specific surface area of ACNT was 320.6 m2 g−1, which was 1.6 times higher than that of pristine CNT (199.0 m2 g−1). The total pore volume of ACNT was 2.71 cm3 g−1, which was approximately 1.4 times higher than that of CNT (1.96 cm3 g−1) (Fig. 1c, inset). Moreover, the average diameter of pores on ACNT (34.3 nm) was smaller than that on pristine CNT (52.8 nm), which confirmed the presence of a higher number of smaller-sized mesopores on ACNT. The pretreatment process created wrinkles along with defects, vacancies, and distortions on the carbon surface, which increased the BET-specific surface area and generated specific pore properties. All the BET results are summarized in Table S1.

Physical and chemical properties of carbon structure before and after pretreatment can also be analyzed by Raman spectroscopy. Fig. 1d shows the Raman spectra of pristine CNT and ACNT. Three major peaks at ∼ 1347, ∼1575, and ∼ 2685 cm−1 were observed, which corresponded to D, G, and 2D bands, respectively. In general, the degree of defects and distortions can be identified by the ratio between D and G bands (ID/IG ), wherein a higher ratio indicates more intense defects in the material. The ID/IG of the pretreated ACNT (ID/IG = 1.87) was approximately twice than that of the untreated CNT (ID/IG = 0.95). This ratio is significantly higher than that achieved by general acid pretreatment or heteroatom doping (ID/IG = ≤1.50), and shows that active sites on the surface of ACNT can be drastically expanded to maximize its catalytic activities. On the other hand, D’ band (1621 cm−1) was also observed, which was partially merged with the G band (Fig. 1d, inset). This band was frequently observed in graphene doped with a heteroatom, such as sulphur [47], and may be associated with oxygen doping in pretreated ACNT.

N-doped CDs were synthesized to form an NCD/ACNT carbon hybrid (Fig. 2 a). The TEM image shows that the size of the NCD is uniform at 5.6 ± 0.5 nm. ACNT and NCD were hybridized by the formation of a carbon–carbon hybrid structure (Fig. 2b). Cu was added to the NCD/ACNT structure to overcome the high ORR activation energy at the cathode of the fuel cells. Cu can spontaneously form CuO/CuO2 nanostructure in the presence of CD as a reducing agent [48]. Therefore, we introduced Cu precursor to the prepared NCD and ACNT solution to induce a spontaneous reduction of Cu ions. In a preliminary study, CuCl2 (concentration of 10 mM), among various concentrations of 0.5, 10, 20, and 30 mM, showed the highest ORR catalytic performance (Fig. S1) and was selected as the optimal condition. For comparison, the same experiment was performed to determine whether Cu nanoparticles were formed without NCD (denoted as Cu/ACNT). As shown in Fig. 2c, no distinct Cu particles were observed on the surface of the carbon support, but a trace amount of Cu was detected by XRD and XPS analysis. This can be attributed to the reaction that took place at the boiling point of water and Cu2+ cation absorption by the negative charge on the activated CNT surface. We hypothesize the Cu particle formation process proceeds as follows. Initially, NCD, ACNT and Cu2+/Cu(OH)2 are well dispersed in water, and the negatively charged NCD and ACNT electrostatically bind with the doubly charged Cu ions and facilitate copper reduction. CuO and CuO2 particles were formed in this step. Simultaneously, a combination of NCD and ACNT to prepare the NCD/ACNT hybrids was also formed. In a control experiment to investigate the clustering of the copper particles, no distinct Cu particles were observed in the presence of ACNT only. Subsequently, particles or crystalline Cu/CuO are formed through a calcination process. Fig. 2d-i and S2 show the TEM image and HAADF-STEM elemental mappings of Cu/NCD/ACNT. The image shows that the size of Cu nanoparticles is approximately 156.6 ± 31.3 nm. Notably, Cu nanoparticles were sparsely formed with NCD as a reducing agent, while their contribution to the ORR catalytic activity was higher than that of NCD/ACNT. This can be attributed to an increase in conductivity of the electrocatalyst with the introduction of Cu, which led to an improvement in electron transfer response. Moreover, N doping on carbon structure lowers the electron density of adjacent carbon atoms and can increase the O2 introduction of the catalytic surface during early reactions of the ORR [48]. The details will be discussed in the section for the evaluation of electrocatalytic performance.

Fig. 3 a shows the XRD patterns of the Cu/NCD/ACNT, Cu/ACNT, and NCD/ACNT samples. The common pattern in three different catalysts, 2θ = 25.8°, corresponds to the (002) crystal index of graphite. Cu/NCD/ACNT catalyst shows three characteristic peaks at 2θ = 43.3°, 50.4°, and 74.1°, which correspond to the crystalline planes (111), (200), and (220) of Cu (JCPDS no. 85–1326), respectively [49]. No impurity peak indicating cuprous or cupric oxides was observed. In contrast, Cu/ACNT catalyst showed weak XRD patterns of Cu2O, confirming the presence of a trace amount of copper oxidants. The crystallite size of Cu for Cu/NCD/ACNT estimated using Scherrer’s equation from peak (111) was ∼ 34.8 nm. In relation to the TEM results, the grain size was different, owing to the difference between the mean crystallite size and the grain size. Fig. 3b shows the FT-IR spectra of Cu/NCD/ACNT, Cu/ACNT, and NCD/ACNT. Two parallel bands at 2990 and 2900 cm−1 represent the symmetric and asymmetric stretching of the CH2 group, –CH3, and = CH groups, respectively. The signal intensities of these bands appear the weakest in the Cu/NCD/ACNT catalyst than those in the NCD/ACNT and Cu/ACNT catalysts, which indicates interactions between Cu nanoparticle and unpaired electron couple of the nitrogen atom. Peaks at 1500–1600 cm−1 correspond to CC and CN bond vibrations, confirming successful doping of N at sp2 network of NCD/ACNT [50]. Since all samples were investigated after calcination under N2 and high temperature, no broad band at 3500–3000 cm−1 corresponding to –OH stretching was observed. The broad and intense peak at 1067 cm−1 corresponds to CO and CN vibrations, respectively, indicating the presence of remaining oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on the NCD/ACNT surface [51].

The structural properties and surface oxidation state of the Cu/NCD/ACNT hybrid were analyzed by XPS. For comparison, the same XPS measurements were performed for NCD/ACNT and Cu/ACNT catalysts. Fig. 4 a and Table S2 summarize the XPS and element content ratio of the three different catalysts. C 1 s peaks were analyzed to confirm the increased catalytic activities and bonding state of the carbon support in each catalyst (Fig. 4b). A total of 4C 1 s peaks were detected, corresponding to CC (284.5 eV), CN (285.5 eV), CO (286.6 eV), and CO (288.2 eV) [52]. In all samples, the main peak for CC binding was at 284.5 eV. In addition, Cu/NCD/ACNT and NCD/ACNT catalyst samples had distinct CN (285.5 eV) peaks with higher binding energy than that of the main peak. In contrast, only CO (286.6 eV) and CO (288.2 eV) were observed on the curve fitting in Cu/ACNT catalyst sample without NCD, which was different from the Cu/NCD/ACNT and NCD/ACNT catalysts. Fig. 4c shows the XPS analysis result of N 1 s, clearly demonstrating the absence of nitrogen in Cu/ACNT catalyst. However, in NCD/ACNT and Cu/NCD/ACNT samples, the peaks corresponding to pyridinic N (398.5 eV), pyrrolic N (399.5 eV), graphitic N (401.1), and oxidized N (402 ∼ 404 eV) were distinctive [53]. In particular, pyridinic N is the center of active sites of the ORR [54,55], and an increase in graphitic N doping leads to excellent ORR activity [56]. Hence, increased ORR catalytic activities may be expected from the binding between NCD and ACNT. N-doped CD has intrinsic effects on catalytic activity and also plays an important role as a reducing agent for Cu2+ during the process of hybrid formation [43]. Fig. 4d shows that although Cu/ACNT and Cu/NCD/ACNT were synthesized under identical conditions, there is almost no Cu 2p peak intensity in Cu/ACNT. To quantify the Cu content of the hybrid catalysts, we compared the analytical results obtained by ICP-OES and XPS for Cu/NCD/ACNT. The detailed ICP-OES and XPS results are summarized in Table S2. Based on XPS analysis, the Cu content of Cu/NCD/ACNT was estimated to be 6.3 wt%, similar to the ICP-OES measurement. On the other hand, the main peaks, 932.5 and 952.4 eV, found on 2p confirmed the presence of Cu metal on the Cu catalyst surface, and the peak with relatively low intensity at 935 eV and the two satellite peaks at 943.7 eV and 962.8 eV confirmed the presence of CuO [57]. Therefore, it was possible to synthesize a Cu/NCD/ACNT hybrid with Cu using N-doped CDs as a reducing agent. Moreover, catalytic effects on ORR activity increased upon N doping and binding with Cu. ORR activity is further discussed in the next section.

To evaluate the ORR electrochemical activity, Cu/NCD/ACNT, NCD/ACNT, and Cu/ACNT samples were subjected to the measurement of catalytic activity by linear-sweep voltammetry (LSV) in an O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. Commercialized Pt/C was used for the comparison of catalytic performance. As shown in Fig. 5 a and S3, the catalyst with the highest onset potential (E onset) was Cu/NCD/ACNT (E onset = 0.921 V), followed by NCD/ACNT (Eonset = 0.869 V) and Cu/ACNT (Eonset = 0.821 V). This shows that the Cu/NCD/ACNT hybrid exhibited the highest ORR activity, except for Pt/C (E onset = 0.944 V). The same tendency was found for half-wave potential (E 1/2). The ORR kinetics at each electrode can be evaluated by considering the Koutecky-Levich (K-L) equation (see ESI). Fig. S4 presents the K-L plots for the catalysts based on the RDE data in Fig. 5a and S3. The n values for the catalysts were calculated form the slopes of the K-L plots using the RDE current densities at 0.3 V (vs. RHE), which were 3.91, 3.76, 3.40, and 3.34 for the Pt/C, Cu/NCD/ACNT, NCD/ACNT, and Cu/ACNT catalysts, respectively. Fig. 5b shows the catalytic properties of CNT and ACNT in the presence of Cu/NCD. Cu/NCD/ACNT (E 1/2 = 0.844 V vs. RHE) showed approximately 22 mV lower overpotential than that of Cu/NCD/CNT (E 1/2 = 0.822 V vs. RHE), indicating a superior catalytic ORR property.

Tafel plots of the catalysts were extrapolated from the corresponding RDE data; the results are shown in Fig. S5a. The Tafel slope was calculated as −79.6, −53.3, −58.7, and −65.9 mV dec−1 for Pt/C, Cu/NCD/ACNT, NCD/ACNT, and Cu/ACNT, respectively. Indeed, Cu/NCD/ACNT exhibited the smallest Tafel slope in the all catalysts, suggesting considerably faster reaction kinetics. Moreover, Cu nanoparticles were introduced to NCD/ACNT to induce intrinsic effects on the catalytic activity of the hybrid. Fig. S5b shows the cyclic voltammogram of the Cu/NCD/ACNT catalyst in an N2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution in the range of −0.14 V to 1.26 V (scan rate of 50 mV s−1), which was performed to examine the presence of Cu nanoparticle and electrochemical behavior. Coupled peak potentials corresponding to the typical Cu oxidation (A and B) and reduction (C and D) were observed. The first peak A (0.63 V vs. RHE) and B (0.84 V vs. RHE) in the anodic direction was caused by 2Cu + 2OH− → Cu2O + 2e− + H2O (Cu(0) → Cu(I)) and Cu2O + 2OH− → 2CuO + H2O + 2e− (Cu(I) → Cu(II)), respectively. Peaks C and D are peak potentials corresponding to a reduction of CuO and Cu2O, respectively [58,59]. Such CV results of Cu nanoparticles show that metal nanoparticles present in various oxidation states could change electron density at active sites during catalytic activity and form favorable conditions for catalytic reaction, which play an important role in the electrocatalytic performance of the ORR [59].

To evaluate and compare the catalytic properties of the synthesized catalysts, the RRDE technique was used to calculate the yield of peroxide (HO2 −) intermediate and the number of electron transfers (n) at 0.3 V (vs. RHE). As shown in Fig. 5c,d and Table 1 , excluding the commercial Pt/C (n = 3.98), Cu/NCD/ACNT (n = 3.84) hybrid exhibited the highest n in the 4e− ORR. Intermediate HO2 −% was calculated by 200×(I r/N)/((I d + I r)/N), where I d and I r represent ring and disk in the RRDE (Fig. 5a,c), and N indicates collection efficiency. The decrease of formed the ORR intermediate, peroxide, also showed similar trends in terms of the n-value increase in the order of Cu/ACNT (22.1%) < NCD/ACNT (15.3%) < Cu/NCD/ACNT (7.9%) < Pt/C (1.0%). Indeed, this provides an estimation that the ORR on proposed Cu/NCD/ACNT nanostructure may have followed a direct 4-electron transfer pathway. The overall results showed that the N-doped carbon structure combined with Cu exhibited the highest ORR catalytic activity in the cathode of the fuel cell. Table S3 shows a list of several reported Cu-based catalysts with various structural features. Catalytic activity is greatly influenced by the properties of catalyst structure as well as electroactive surface area. Fig. 5e shows the plots of the capacitive current density as a function of the scan rate, and the slope can be used to estimate the active surface area of the catalyst. Capacitance per active surface area of all catalysts was higher in the order of Cu/NCD/ACNT (Cdl = 21.31 mF cm−2) > NCD/ACNT (Cdl = 20.65 mF cm−2) > Cu/ACNT (14.23 mF cm−2), which is similar to the ORR catalytic activity. Results for ACNT (12.36 mF cm−2) and CNT (Cdl = 9.29 mF cm−2) are shown in Fig. S6. The slope value was slightly higher in Cu/NCD/ACNT than in NCD/ACNT. This is because a specific surface area is dominated by the NCD/ACNT structure, while a distribution of produced Cu nanoparticles in Cu/NCD/ACNT is limited. Hence, this shows that the ORR activity of each catalyst is not only induced by an increase in specific surface area but also affected by the introduction of Cu, which increases electrical conductivity and electron transfer rate. This means that the direct pathway converting ORR intermediates into the OH− product plays an important role. Fig. 5f shows methanol resistance at the cathode measured by chronoamperometric i-t method at 0.8 V (vs. RHE) with the addition of 1.0 M methanol into N2 –saturated 0.1 M KOH. In a DMFC, the transfer of methanol from an anode to an adjacent cathode can decrease the efficiency of the fuel cell, and hence methanol tolerance is an important assessment factor. The commercial Pt/C catalyst showed a large response to oxidation currents in an O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution with methanol, whereas Cu/NCD/ACNT hybrid showed high tolerance to methanol. Taken together, it can be concluded that Cu/NCD/ACNT with higher methanol tolerance has a similar level of catalytic activity to Pt/C.

The kinetics of the electrode of each catalyst sample was analyzed through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The charge-transfer resistance values (R ct) estimated from the semi-circle at low frequency (high Z') showed that Cu/NCD/ACNT exhibits higher charge-transfer efficiency than the other catalysts (Fig. 6 a). The charge-transfer resistance values for all catalysts were in the order ACNT (Z' = 713.83) > Cu/ACNT (Z' = 679.13) > NCD/ACNT (Z' = 561.3) > Cu/NCD/ACNT (Z' = 394.1). Cu/ACNT synthesized without the NCD barely formed metal Cu nanoparticles; hence, it showed EIS results similar to those of ACNT. In contrast, the Cu/NCD/ACNT hybrid largely reduced the charge-transfer resistance and thereby had stronger catalytic effects on ORR activity than those of NCD/ACNT or Cu/ACNT. To assess the long-term durability of the Cu/NCD/ACNT hybrid, chronoamperometry was conducted to compare the durability of the Cu/NCD/ACNT hybrid with those of commercial Pt/C and NCD/ACNT. Fig. 6b shows chronoamperometric responses measured by the RDE electrode (400 rpm) in an O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution for 10 h. The Pt/C electrode showed a 21% decrease in current density, whereas Cu/NCD/ACNT and NCD/ACNT showed approximately half the reduction with 10%. Although the sum of the current decrease for NCD/ACNT and Cu/NCD/ACNT was found to be the identical over 10 h, the initial current decay for NCD/ACNT was more than that for Cu/NCD/ACNT. Hence, Cu/NCD/ACNT was found to have higher stability in an alkaline solution than commercial Pt/C and NCD/ACNT. To better understand electrode stability and surface properties, CV before and after the stability test for Cu/NCD/ACNT was performed in a solution containing 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3/4− and 0.1 M KCl at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 (Fig. S7). After the stability test, the peak current of Cu/NCD/ACNT decreased by only 7.3%, indicating that the catalyst surface did not significantly change. Moreover, the electron transfer of the Fe(CN)6 3/4− redox couple over Cu/NCD/ACNT is almost identical peak potentials (ΔE p = 92 mV) before and after the stability test. These results demonstrate that the accelerated electrocatalytic reaction of Cu/NCD/ACNT occurs on its surface, highlighting the satisfactory stability of the catalyst. In addition, the redox couples in the vicinity of the Cu/NCD/ACNT electrode can readily transfer electrons to the exposed electrode surface, resulting in enhanced electrocatalytic properties.

To find an alternative to precious metal catalysts at the cathode of a fuel cell, Cu/NCD/ACNT sample was formed by introducing NCD and Cu to pretreated ACNT. The enhanced performance of Cu/NCD/ACNT catalysts was attributed to the N-doped CD/ACNT hybrid structure, including the advanced pretreatment effect of the pristine CNT, providing more active edges and defect sites, which play an important role in the electrocatalytic performance of the ORR. As part of an ongoing study on the assembly of NCD/ACNT nanostructures, it was envisioned that the NCD/ACNT could offer advantages unique to hybrid structures, i.e. providing a large surface to volume ratio with high density without significant alteration of the size and morphology. In particular, a large increase in the specific surface area of pretreated ACNT (320.6 m2 g−1) in relation to that of untreated CNT (199.0 m2 g−1) was observed in BET measurements. The defects and distortions on the pretreated ACNT surface (ID/IG = 1.87) measured by Raman spectroscopy increased by approximately two-fold in relation to those on the untreated CNT surface. The Cu/NCD/ACNT hybrid catalyst showed overpotential as low as ΔE 1/2 = 22 mV. The electrical resistance of Cu/NCD/ACNT hybrid was confirmed to be greatly reduced in EIS measurements. According to the electrochemical analysis, the changes in structural properties induced by the introduction of NCD caused changes in electron density adjacent to N, and greatly improved the ORR catalytic activity. Cu/NCD/ACNT hybrid also showed superior methanol tolerance as well as considerably higher catalytic activity in 10 h stability measurements than Pt/C. The above results suggest that Cu/NCD/ACNT hybrid should have the potential to replace the currently commercialized Pt/C as a material for fuel cell electrodes.

Jaemun Kim: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Sunguk Noh: Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation. Jun Ho Shim: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Daegu University Research Grant, 2020.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2023.117423.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
